text: My port placement is very simple. If it's an obese versus thin patient. If it's a thin patient, the camera port goes below the umbilicus. If it's an obese patient, it goes above the umbilicus. The more obese, the higher up it goes. I place my right lateral port first. Usually there's a visual inspection of where the gallbladder and liver junction occur. About two centimeters below this give me enough access to retract superiorly. That's where I place this port. I then split the difference for the middle port. The left upper quadrant port, usually an eyeball in the general location of the middle port, kind of mirroring its location. Port placement is very important as this can impede your progress throughout the case. Now, if you did have to place a system port for a very difficult gallbladder, especially in an obese male where you have to retract some of that fat or momentum purely, you can either place it between the lateral and middle port or middle and camera port, depending on adequate exposure.

The surgeon demonstrates dissection techniques, highlighting the removal or clipping of a lymph node to improve exposure of the cystic duct and cystic artery. This maneuver is shown to facilitate clearer access to these structures during challenging gallbladder cases.

Of course, the goal is to get a critical view in every case and I will attempt to do so in every single case. However, occasionally it's just not possible or proceeding to blindly get that critical view can actually make it more harder or decrease your safety. I say this not necessarily to deviate from standard practice, but you know, there's not a hundred percent guarantee in everything. The goal is to get a critical view and do a safety section. And that's what I try to do in most every case. I usually take the artery first as I think that it gives you more exposure for the more important structure, which I think is the cystic duct and surrounding ducts.

The surgeon uses a pro grasp to retract the liver, elevating it to allow the gallbladder to retract inferiorly. A dome down technique is employed from the top, providing clear access to the dissection plane. The vessels are visible, and the dissection proceeds down to the mesentery pedicle, which will be clipped at the end.
The surgeon performs a close inspection of the gallbladder fossa using the Firefly imaging system, ensuring there is no visible bleeding or bile leakage.

Occasionally you just cannot dissect out the critical view inferiorly, or you just cannot see the anatomy like you like. Don't be afraid to do a dome down technique. And the last thing standing is usually your cystic duct. Also frequent use of the ICG will help guide your dissection. Not necessarily in this case, but if you have a very sick gallbladder, you can see the posterior wall and gallbladder junction with the Firefly that can help dissect to make sure you're in the correct space.
